title: Samsung RF28R Refrigerator
prompt:
Samsung RF28R7201SR 36" French Door Refrigerator — back view exact, cables as separate parts

Model the Samsung RF28R7201SR 4-door French Door refrigerator (28 cu. ft.,
stainless). Product page for grounding:
https://appliancetvoutlet.com/product/samsung-rf28r7201sr-french-door/
Published dimensions (cite in provenance): exterior width 35.75 in
(~908 mm), height 70 in (~1778 mm), depth 36.5 in (~927 mm). Search the
web for front/side product photos of this exact model for the front
styling (flat stainless doors, recessed pocket handles / EZ-Open freezer
handle, external water dispenser on the left French door, Samsung wordmark).

## PRIORITY: the back view must match the attached photo exactly

The attached `reference_back.png` (rear view of this model) is the
canonical reference for the ENTIRE back of the unit, and the primary
deliverable of this run. Reproduce it faithfully — every visible feature:

- Embossed galvanized-steel back panel: the large stamped rectangular
  relief fields (big upper field with rounded corners, smaller stamped
  panels), the recessed central service pocket with its black switch/valve
  box, and the screwed lower-left access plate with its own stamped relief.
- Top-right junction/inlet box where the power cord and water line exit,
  with the cord clipped/taped down the right side.
- Lower machine compartment: full-width bottom panel with the louvered
  vent grille (bottom-left, dense slats — real cuts, not decals), stamped
  model/compliance text band, yellow warning label lower-center-right,
  small white/red service labels along the cable runs.
- Leveling feet / rollers at the base.

## Cables and tubes — SEPARATE PARTS (hard requirement)

Every cable/tube visible on the back must be modeled as its own top-level
part (own collection, own outline node) — NOT merged into the body — with
non-destructive curve-based construction so each can be re-routed. From
the photo, at minimum:

1. `power_cord` — black cable from the junction box, routed down and
   across to the lower-left, ending in a grounded plug.
2. `water_line_blue` — the blue tube running from the junction box down
   the right side with taped/clipped segments (model the small tape flags
   and clips as body-owned details).
3. `water_tube_white` — the thin white/clear tube looping across the
   central lower back to the left side.

Rules for these parts (this pipeline has been burned before — encode them
as deterministic constraints in the spec):

- The hard cabinet bounding-box constraints (908 x 927 x 1778 mm) apply to
  the CABINET ONLY (body + closed doors + drawers + plinth). The cables,
  tubes, cord and their clips are EXTERNAL REAR ACCESSORIES: exclude them
  from every cabinet dimension measurement, and allow them to sit proud of
  the rear face by up to 40 mm. Do NOT flatten or embed them into the back
  panel to satisfy a bbox check.
- Emit non_intersection constraints: each cable/tube vs body, and each
  cable/tube vs the other cables/tubes (they cross visually but must not
  interpenetrate — maintain >= 1 mm separation where they cross).
- Emit clearance (rests_on-style, max_gap ~2 mm) constraints where the
  cables are taped/clipped against the back panel, so they read as
  attached, not floating.
- These parts will later be used for a cable-TAPING simulation: keep each
  cable a single continuous curve-driven mesh with an editable path, so a
  future run can articulate/detach segments. Fixed (no joints) this run.

## Articulation (standard for this product)

- `door_left_hinge`, `door_right_hinge` — revolute, the two French doors
  (0 to ~110 deg outward).
- `flex_drawer_slide` — prismatic, the FlexZone middle drawer.
- `freezer_drawer_slide` — prismatic, the bottom freezer drawer.
- Interior detail may stay minimal (basic shelving volumes); exterior and
  the back are the focus.

## Everything else

Front/side per the product photos you find: flat stainless panels, dark
gray toe kick, external ice/water dispenser recess in the left door,
door gaps ~4 mm, Samsung wordmark decal. Real boolean cuts for all vents
and recesses; no forbidden objects (no food, no interior clutter, no
water). Renders must include a straight-on rear view and rear
three-quarter views matching `reference_back.png` framing so the critics
can diff the back directly against the photo.

title: Samsung WA50R Washer
prompt:
Samsung WA50R5200AW Top-Load Washer — with drain-hose assembly articulation

Model the washer shown in the reference photos (Samsung 5.0 cu. ft. top-load
washer, model WA50R5200AW, white). The reference photos are canonical for
proportions, part placement, control layout, side stamping, rear layout, and
tub texture.

## Articulation (three joints total)

Keep the two standard articulations from the product:

1. `lid_hinge` — revolute; the top lid opens rearward (0 to ~105 deg).
2. `cycle_selector_rotation` — revolute; the chrome control knob/dial spins
   about the console face normal.

The detergent drawer may be modeled as fixed geometry; its slide articulation
is NOT required for this run.

3. **`drain_hose_insertion` — the focus of this run.** The rear drain hose is
   a SEPARATE moving link with a prismatic assembly DOF that seats it into
   retaining clips on the back of the machine (details below).

## Drain hose + clips (primary deliverable)

### Hose geometry

- Gray corrugated flexible drain hose, routed as in the canonical rear photo:
  it emerges near the top-center outlet, forms a HIGH INVERTED U / wide arch
  across the upper rear, and descends down the left side (viewer's left in the
  rear photo), ending in a smooth, slightly wider gray cuff/nozzle near the
  bottom. Evenly spaced annular ribs with darker valleys; parameterize
  `drain_hose_outer_diameter_mm` (~32), `drain_hose_corrugation_pitch_mm`
  (~12), and the path curve.
- The hose is one rigid link for simulation purposes (no soft-body/flexible
  sim required). Build it non-destructively (curve + procedural corrugation)
  so the path stays editable.

### Retaining clips ("clippers") on the back panel

- The rear of the machine carries THREE hose retaining C-clips, fixed to the
  body/rear panel (part of the `body` link, e.g. an object named
  `hose_clips`): one near the top outlet where the arch begins, one at the
  left shoulder of the arch, and one mid-way down the left descent.
- Each clip is a C-profile saddle sized to the hose: inner radius = hose
  outer radius + ~0.5 mm clearance, mouth opening slightly narrower than the
  hose diameter (snap-fit look), with a small mounting pad/boss against the
  rear surface. Model ONE clip and instance it (linked duplicates or GN
  instancing); clips must sit exactly on the hose path so the seated hose
  rests concentrically in all three saddles.
- The clips are mandatory, permanent geometry. Do not remove or replace them
  in later revision passes.

### Assembly articulation + simulation

- Joint: `drain_hose_insertion`, prismatic, parent link = body root link,
  child link = `drain_hose`. Axis points away from the rear face (world +Y),
  so the DOF pulls the hose straight out of the clip mouths.
  - DOF value 0 (rest pose) = fully SEATED: hose held in all three clips,
    matching the rear reference photo.
  - DOF max (~0.10 m) = DETACHED: the whole hose hovers clear of the clips
    and the rear panel by at least 60 mm.
- Deliverables for the assembly sim:
  - A 120-frame insertion animation (detached -> seated -> detached sweep)
    rendered from a rear three-quarter camera, plus the usual lid/dial
    articulation previews.
  - A per-frame collision sweep across the full insertion range: at NO frame
    may the hose interpenetrate the body, rear service panel, power cord, or
    plinth. At the seated pose the hose must CONTACT the three clip saddles
    (rests_on, gap <= 1 mm, penetration <= 1 mm) without penetrating them.
    Report the per-frame minimum clearances.
  - USD export must contain all three joints (2 revolute + 1 prismatic
    insertion) so the assembly can be driven in Isaac Sim.

## Envelope rules — read carefully (this fixes a known failure mode)

- The hard cabinet bounding box 699 mm (X) x 747 mm (Y) x 1132 mm (Z) applies
  to the CABINET ONLY (body, lid closed, console, plinth).
- The drain hose, hose clips, power cord, and inlet fittings are EXTERNAL
  REAR ACCESSORIES. They hang proud of the rear face, exactly as in the rear
  reference photo, and MAY exceed the 747 mm depth by up to 60 mm. Do NOT
  flatten, embed, or sink the hose into the body to satisfy a depth check.
- Structurally: keep `drain_hose` as its own top-level collection/link — do
  not nest it inside the `body` collection — and scope every cabinet
  dimension constraint so it measures cabinet geometry only (exclude hose,
  clips, and cord from the measured set).
- Emit deterministic spec constraints for: hose-vs-body non_intersection,
  hose-vs-rear-panel non_intersection, hose-rests_on-clips clearance at the
  seated pose, and a count check that exactly three clip instances exist.

## Everything else

Match the reference photos as in a standard run: white glossy cabinet with
stamped side panels, black plinth, recessed top deck with smoked-glass lid,
satin console with dial/buttons/LED, stainless perforated tub with low
pulsator, rear service panel with vents, red/blue inlet valves, and black
power cord down the right side. Real boolean cuts for all openings; no
forbidden objects (front-loader door, agitator post, laundry, water).

title: Louis Vuitton Bisten 55
prompt:
Louis Vuitton Bisten 55 — vintage hard monogram suitcase

Model the vintage Louis Vuitton Bisten 55 hard-sided suitcase shown in
the attached listing photos (9 images: front, back, top, bottom, sides,
corner/latch close-ups, interior). The attached photos are canonical for
proportions, hardware placement, wear-free geometry, and the monogram
scale/orientation. Ground the published dimensions from the web (Bisten
55 is ~55 cm wide; find the height/depth from LV or reseller specs and
cite them; unpublished values stay named parameters).

## THE MONOGRAM — precise, iconic, non-negotiable

The shell is LV Monogram coated canvas, and the pattern must be the
EXACT iconic motif set, not an approximation:
- The staggered repeating grid of four elements: the interlocked "LV"
  initials, the quatrefoil (four-lobed flower inside a circle), the
  four-petal flower (pointed diamond/star form), and the negative
  four-petal flower inside a filled circle.
- Obtain the authentic pattern as a vector/SVG or high-resolution tile:
  search the web for "Louis Vuitton monogram pattern SVG" and download
  one (curl via Bash is available; convert SVG to a high-res PNG tile
  for the texture atlas). If no usable vector can be downloaded,
  reconstruct the tile programmatically (PIL script, like the printed
  label atlases used in prior runs) matching the reference close-ups
  motif by motif.
- Colors: dark chocolate-brown ground with tan/ochre motifs. Tile it at
  the scale visible in the listing photos (measure motif spacing
  relative to the 55 cm width) with the classic diagonal-stagger layout,
  continuous across each face, applied as an image texture with subtle
  bump (coated canvas grain). The monogram is a TEXTURE on the shell,
  never modeled geometry.

## Body and hardware (per the photos)

- Two-half hard shell (lid shallower than the base), rounded edges.
- Lozine trim: the light beige/tan binding strips along every shell edge,
  fixed with evenly spaced small brass rivets (procedural rivet rows).
- Brass hardware: four rounded corner caps on each face-side corners,
  two spring latch clasps flanking a central S-lock plate under the
  handle, all on the front face; brass hinges along the bottom rear.
- Leather handle: rounded brown leather grip mounted on two brass loop
  anchors, top center.
- Interior: simple beige lining volume with cross straps (minimal).

## Articulation

1. `lid_hinge` — revolute along the rear bottom edge: the lid opens
   0 to ~100 deg like a classic trunk.
2. `clasp_left`, `clasp_right` — revolute: each spring latch lever flips
   open/closed (~0-60 deg about its pivot pin).
3. The lid must not intersect the base, lozine trim, or hardware at any
   hinge angle; the clasps must clear the lock plate through their range.

Include the demo_articulation muted-NLA bake per the standard convention.

## Constraints to encode

- Hard bbox from cited published dims; the handle protrudes above the
  shell — treat the shell envelope and handle separately.
- Latch/lock/corner hardware counts (2 clasps, 1 lock, 8 corner caps).
- Rivet rows must be real instanced geometry (count check on a sensible
  row pattern), not texture dots.
- non_intersection: lid vs base across the hinge sweep; handle vs shell.
- No forbidden objects: no clothes, no stickers/travel labels, no straps
  beyond the interior cross straps, no scene props.

title: Bambu Lab P1S Combo
prompt:
Bambu Lab P1S 3D Printer Combo (P1S + AMS)

Model the **Bambu Lab P1S 3D printer Combo**: the enclosed CoreXY printer
with the **AMS (Automatic Material System)** docked on top, as sold.
Research the official product pages (https://bambulab.com / 
https://store.bambulab.com/products/p1s) and press/review photos for exact
appearance and dimensions. This is a modern consumer machine — get the
proportions and signature details right.

## Product identity (verify against official sources)

- P1S body: enclosed cube, approx 389 × 389 × 458 mm, matte dark-gray /
  black sheet-metal shell with black plastic trim; front face has a large
  framed glass door and, above it, the control area (2.7" mono LCD +
  navigation buttons on the right, front-facing).
- Front glass door: dark tinted glass panel with black frame, hinged on
  the RIGHT edge, magnetic latch on the left; verify hinge side from
  photos.
- Top: glass lid panel sitting in a recess (removable in reality); the
  AMS unit sits on top of it in the combo configuration.
- AMS: approx 368 × 283 × 251 mm glossy/matte light-gray body with a
  transparent smoked hinged lid, holding 4 filament spools side by side;
  4 PTFE outlet fittings at the rear feeding one bundled tube.
- Side panels: solid; right side carries the power socket/switch at rear
  bottom. Rear: cable, vent grid, PTFE tube run from AMS to the toolhead
  inlet.
- Interior: CoreXY motion system — toolhead with gray shroud on carbon/
  steel X rails at the top of the chamber, moving in X and Y; heatbed
  (textured black PEI plate on silver bed frame) that travels the full
  Z height; chamber floor with purge chute at rear-left front... verify
  from photos; flexible cable chain to the toolhead.
- Build volume 256 × 256 × 256 mm — the bed and gantry travel must be
  consistent with this.
- Spools: 4 standard 1-kg spools (~200 mm diameter, ~65 mm wide) resting
  on roller shafts inside the AMS.

## Articulation (sim-ready joints, Level-3 USD export)

- `front_door_hinge`: revolute, 0 (closed) to ~120° open.
- `top_lid_lift`: model the glass top lid as a link with a small prismatic
  lift (0–30 mm) OR fixed-detachable with dock markers — pick prismatic so
  it is a real DOF.
- `ams_lid_hinge`: revolute, transparent lid opens ~100° toward the rear.
- `spool_1_spin` .. `spool_4_spin`: revolute, continuous, each spool
  rotates about its horizontal axis.
- `toolhead_x`: prismatic, ±128 mm along X (toolhead on the X carriage).
- `gantry_y`: prismatic, ±128 mm along Y (X-rail assembly on Y rails).
- `bed_z`: prismatic, 0 (top, near nozzle) to −256 mm (bottom), the bed
  link travels down.
- Kinematic chain: toolhead is child of the X-rail link, X-rail link is
  child of the body; bed is child of the body. Spools are children of the
  AMS body link; AMS body is fixed on the printer top.

Rest state: door closed, AMS lid closed, bed at mid height, toolhead
centered, spools loaded (model 4 spools in different filament colors).

## Conventions (mandatory)

- Every joint's state property (clamped slider, degrees or millimeters)
  lives ON THE ROOT LINK OBJECT (the printer body), driven via drivers —
  NOT on the Scene datablock.
- After building the rig, force a full depsgraph evaluation
  (`view_layer.update()` + `evaluated_depsgraph_get()`) before any
  reparenting-dependent step, and verify every driver reports `is_valid`;
  an invalid driver is a build failure.
- Bake a muted-NLA `demo_articulation` action on the root link object:
  door open/close, AMS lid open, one spool spin, then a printing motion
  demo (bed descends while toolhead sweeps X/Y like a first-layer
  pattern).

## Visual details that sell the model

- "Bambu Lab" wordmark on the front door frame and AMS lid.
- Door/window tint: dark smoked glass with slight transparency showing
  the interior.
- Chamfered top edges of the shell; recessed vent slots on rear.
- Toolhead: gray angular shroud with front cooling-fan grille.
- PEI plate texture and gold-colored "Bambu" bed clips detail if visible.
- Filament spools in 4 distinct colors (e.g. green, orange, white, black)
  with wound-filament banding texture.
- PTFE tube: rear bundle from AMS to toolhead with a natural sag — model
  as a smooth curve with slack, non-intersecting with lid/gantry travel.

## Deterministic constraints to encode

- Overall dims of printer body and AMS (±3% of published).
- Build-volume consistency: bed_z travel ≥ 256 mm; toolhead_x and
  gantry_y travel ≥ 256 mm each.
- Door and AMS-lid joint limits; spool joints continuous.
- Non-intersection: door vs body through the full hinge range; AMS lid vs
  spools; toolhead vs shell across full X/Y travel; bed vs chamber floor
  at bottom of travel.
- 4 spools present, each with a spin joint.

title: Le Creuset Ramekins
prompt: Model Le Creuset Stackable Ramekins in Sea Salt: stoneware ramekin with a straight-wall stackable design, rounded lip, shallow bowl, and foot ring; three thin decoration lines on the side with an embossed LE CREUSET logo, and a sea-foam green ceramic glaze with an ivory interior.

title: TowerPro SG90 Servo
prompt:
TowerPro SG90 9g micro hobby servo — accurate parts + assembly-from-explosion animation

Model the classic **TowerPro SG90 9-gram micro hobby servo** with datasheet-
accurate dimensions and specs, full internal parts, and a demo animation
that starts from an EXPLODED layout and assembles the servo, then sweeps
the output horn with the gear train spinning at correct ratios.

## Research mandate (accuracy is the point of this asset)

Verify every dimension and spec against the TowerPro SG90 datasheet and
published teardowns before modeling. Record each value with its source in
`docs/sg90_spec.md`. Values to pin down (approximate expected values given
for sanity — the datasheet/teardown wins where they differ):

- Case: 22.2 × 11.8 mm footprint, ~22.7 mm body height, 31–32 mm overall
  height to top of output spline; blue nylon 3-piece housing (bottom cap,
  middle body, top gearbox cover) held by 2 long self-tapping screws from
  the bottom.
- Mounting flanges: overall span ~32.2 mm, two ~2 mm screw holes at
  ~27.8–28 mm spacing, flange thickness ~2.5 mm.
- Output spline: 21-tooth, ~4.8 mm outer diameter, ~3 mm tall; M2 horn
  screw hole in the center.
- Servo horn: model the single-arm horn mounted (arm length ~19–20 mm hub
  to tip, hub Ø~7 mm) plus the M2 mounting screw. Record the horn hole
  pitch. ("Spinner diameter" = horn hub and spline dims — get both right.)
- Cable: 3-core flat ribbon (brown = GND, red = +5 V, orange = signal),
  each conductor ~1.2 mm OD so ribbon ~3.6–4 mm wide × ~1.2 mm thick,
  ~245 mm long, strain-relief exiting the LEFT case side near the bottom,
  ending in a 3-pin JR female connector (2.54 mm pitch, ~7.9 × 2.5 mm
  face). Model the ribbon with a natural sag/curve.
- Specs to record in `docs/sg90_spec.md` and as scene custom properties:
  weight 9 g, stall torque ~1.8 kg·cm @ 4.8 V, speed ~0.1 s/60°,
  operating angle ~180° (±90°), operating voltage 3.0–7.2 V, and the
  GEAR TRAIN tooth counts + overall ratio from a teardown source
  (typical micro-servo overall reduction is in the few-hundreds:1 range —
  find and cite actual tooth counts; compute the exact ratio from them).

## Parts (all as separate links — the explosion needs real parts)

- `bottom_cap` (with the 2 long screws `case_screw_1`, `case_screw_2`)
- `middle_body` (main case with mounting flanges and cable strain relief)
- `pcb` (control board with driver IC, through-hole solder joints, cable
  soldered to it)
- `dc_motor` (small brushed can motor with pinion gear on top)
- `potentiometer` (position feedback pot under the output gear)
- `top_cover` (gearbox cover with output bushing boss)
- Gear train (white POM, each a separate link with correct RELATIVE sizes
  from the teardown tooth counts): `motor_pinion`, `gear_1`, `gear_2`,
  `gear_3` (compound gears on two idler shafts), `output_gear` (with
  integrated output spline and 180° end-stop tab)
- `servo_horn` + `horn_screw`
- `cable_ribbon` + `connector_jr`

Case parting lines, screw bosses, the pot shaft, and gear shafts must
line up so the assembly is physically plausible (shafts pass through gear
bores, screws reach from bottom cap into top cover bosses).

## Articulation (sim-ready, Level-3 USD export)

- `output_shaft_rotate`: revolute, −90° to +90°, on the output gear/spline
  (horn attached).
- `motor_pinion_spin`, `gear_1_spin`, `gear_2_spin`, `gear_3_spin`:
  revolute, continuous, each about its own shaft axis.
- Couple the gear joints to `output_shaft_rotate` with drivers using the
  researched tooth ratios so posing the output slider spins the whole
  train at correct relative speeds (motor pinion fastest). Keep each gear
  joint individually poseable in the USD export (the coupling is a
  Blender-side convenience via drivers, ratios documented).
- Root: `sg90_servo`, root link `middle_body`, root_role `dynamic`.

Conventions (mandatory): joint state sliders (degrees) live ON THE ROOT
LINK OBJECT, not the Scene; drivers forced through a depsgraph evaluation
after rig build and every driver must report `is_valid`.

## Demo animation — `demo_assembly` (muted NLA, on the root link object)

Timeline:
1. Start EXPLODED: parts separated along the assembly axes in a clean
   vertical/radial explosion (bottom cap + screws below; PCB, motor, pot
   at mid levels; middle body central; gears staged above their shafts;
   top cover, horn, horn screw above; cable/connector out to the side).
   Every part remains rigid — offsets on part transforms only.
2. Assemble in real assembly order: motor + pot + PCB into middle body →
   cable seated → gears onto shafts in mesh order → top cover down →
   case screws up through bottom → horn onto spline → horn screw.
3. Finish with a function demo: output sweeps −90° → +90° → 0° with the
   gear train spinning at the coupled ratios.

The SAVED REST STATE of the .blend is the fully ASSEMBLED servo (all
verification constraints are measured there). Also write
`exports/exploded_pose.json` recording each part's exploded-state offset
so the explosion is reproducible.

## Deterministic constraints to encode

- Case footprint, body height, overall height, flange span and hole
  spacing (±2% of the researched datasheet values).
- Spline OD and tooth count; horn arm length; ribbon width/thickness;
  connector pitch (±5%).
- Output joint limits ±90°; gear joints continuous; gear tooth-count
  properties present and ratio-consistent.
- Non-intersection of all parts in the assembled rest state (gears may
  CONTACT at mesh interfaces but not penetrate); screws inside their
  bosses; no part protrudes through the case.
- All drivers valid; sliders on root link object.

## Materials / look

Blue translucent-edge nylon case (SG90 signature blue), white POM gears,
green PCB with visible traces/IC, copper motor can accents, black pot,
brown/red/orange ribbon, gold connector contacts, tan/white nylon horn.
Molded "TowerPro SG90" style label detail on the case side (simple decal).

title: Kraft Real Mayo
prompt:
Kraft Real Mayo "Creamy & Smooth" 30 fl oz jar

Model the **Kraft Real Mayo 30 fl oz (887 mL) round PET jar** exactly as
sold (Safeway listing: https://www.safeway.com/shop/product-details.960038199.html).
The canonical reference is the provided front product photo
`product_photo_front.png` in this inputs folder. Record researched
dimensions and sources in `docs/mayo_spec.md`.

## THE LABEL IS THE POINT — exact extracted image texture

The front label must be the **exact artwork extracted from the reference
photo as a clean image texture** — NOT a procedural re-creation, NOT
re-typeset text, NOT a generated approximation.

Mandatory workflow:
1. Crop the label region from `references/product_photo_front.png`
   (the blue wrap: from the label's top edge below the shoulder to its
   bottom edge above the base flare).
2. Undo the cylindrical foreshortening: the photo shows the label
   wrapped on a cylinder, so horizontally remap (arcsin-based unwarp) so
   the artwork becomes flat/orthographic. Use Python/PIL (or bpy) with a
   proper cylindrical unwrap, not a naive stretch.
3. Clean the result: crop to the visible ~140–160° of wrap, mild
   inpaint/extend at the edges (continue the blue field sideways so the
   texture can wrap the label's full circumference without an obvious
   seam; the photographed art stays front and center, unmodified).
4. Save as `assets/images/label_texture.png` (keep resolution as high
   as the source allows; do not upscale artificially).
5. Apply on a dedicated label geometry band (slightly proud of the jar
   wall, <=0.5 mm offset, conforming to the curvature) via cylindrical
   UV projection matched so the artwork's apparent width on the front
   matches the photo (Kraft ribbon logo, "REAL MAYO" script, "Creamy &
   Smooth", "30 fl oz", CAGE FREE EGGS badge, sandwich image all
   legible and undistorted from a front view).
6. Verify by rendering a front view at the same angle as the photo and
   comparing side by side; iterate the unwarp/UV until the label reads
   identical.

Also add a simple back label (plain white nutrition panel — generic is
fine, no extraction needed).

## Jar anatomy (verify against the photo + listing)

- Round wide-mouth PET jar, ~16–17 cm tall overall, ~10 cm diameter
  (research actual numbers; the 30 oz jar is the classic squat round
  Kraft jar).
- Clear PET walls; the jar LOOKS white because it is full of mayo —
  model the mayo as an interior volume (off-white, subsurface-ish)
  visible through the clear wall, with a small headspace under the lid
  and a subtle fill line visible near the shoulder in grazing light.
- Body: cylindrical with a gentle waist, label band in the middle,
  shoulder dome with rounded transition, wide neck with external
  threads (research thread count ~3 starts on these jars — approximate
  is fine, but model REAL thread geometry, not a smooth cylinder).
- Base: recessed pushup with molded PET recycling mark.
- Cap: the signature royal-blue polypropylene screw cap with fine
  vertical knurling/flutes around the skirt, flat top with a slight
  dome, "Kraft" embossing optional. ~10.5 cm diameter, ~2.5 cm tall.
- Foil liner seal: model a thin foil disc link under the cap (visible
  when the cap is off).

## Articulation (sim-ready, Level-3 USD export)

- `cap_unscrew`: revolute, continuous, cap about the jar axis; thread
  engagement is visual (no translation needed during rotation).
- `cap_lift`: prismatic, 0–30 mm, the cap assembly lifting off after
  unscrewing (separate DOF so simulation can remove the cap).
- `foil_seal`: fixed-detachable link with paired dock markers on the
  jar rim (NOT a revolute/prismatic joint).
- Root: `kraft_mayo_jar`, root link `jar_body`, root_role `dynamic`.

Conventions (mandatory): joint state sliders ON THE ROOT LINK OBJECT,
clamped to limits; drivers forced through a depsgraph evaluation after
rig build; all drivers must report `is_valid`.

## Demo animation — `demo_cap` (muted NLA, on the root link)

1. Cap unscrews ~2 turns while lifting slightly (composite of the two
   DOFs), then lifts clear.
2. Hold showing the foil seal, then reverse: cap lowers and screws back.

Rest state: upright, cap on, on the floor plane.

## Deterministic constraints

- Jar height/diameter and cap diameter within ±3% of researched dims.
- Label band present with image texture from `label_texture.png` (check
  the material uses an image texture node, not procedural color).
- Label conformity: every label vertex within 1 mm of the jar surface.
- Joint limits as specified; cap/jar non-intersection through the full
  unscrew+lift range; mayo volume contained inside the jar wall.
- All drivers valid; sliders on root link object.

## Materials / look

Clear PET (transmission, slight thickness), off-white mayo interior,
royal blue glossy PP cap, crisp label image texture, subtle foil
metallic seal. Match the reference photo's overall read as closely as
possible in the final front render.

title: Serenity Medicine Box
prompt:
Household medicine storage box (cantilever tray chest) — Serenity Blue

Model the AliExpress "Large Capacity Medicine Box, Household Medicine
Storage, Multi-layer Classification" chest
(https://www.aliexpress.us/item/3256805071272002.html) — a large PP
plastic household first-aid/medicine chest with a transparent flip lid
and **cantilever-expanding trays**, like a tackle box. Reference photos
in this folder: `ref_open_cantilever_trays.png` (lid open, trays
scissored out) and `ref_latch_dial_open_lid.png` (front rotary latch
dial, lid raised).

**COLOR OVERRIDE (user request): the opaque body plastic is SERENITY
BLUE** — the soft powder pastel blue of PANTONE 15-3919 (~#B3C7D6,
sRGB ≈ 179,199,214) — replacing the cream/white of the listing photos.
Keep the lid transparent smoke-tinted as photographed; latch dial and
handles in a slightly lighter/whiter tint of the same serenity family.

## Anatomy (from listing photos + spec)

- Listed outer size 50 × 40 × 30 cm — but that is the shipping-listing
  spec for the largest variant; sanity-check proportions against the
  photos (box is clearly wider than deep, roughly 2:1.4:1) and record
  the chosen dimensions in `docs/medicine_box_spec.md`.
- `base_bin`: deep rectangular lower bin (~half the total height),
  rounded vertical corners, slightly tapered draft, molded foot pads.
- Two `tray_lower_left/right` + two `tray_upper_left/right` cantilever
  trays (or one full-width tray per level per photos — match the
  photos: full-width trays with molded divider walls, 7 compartments
  total across levels), each carried by paired scissor links on BOTH
  ends connecting to the bin and to each other (classic cantilever
  tackle-box mechanism): when the lid-side handles are pulled up/back,
  the trays translate up AND outward, staying level.
- `lid`: one-piece transparent smoke-gray lid with a molded top recess
  and integral carry region, hinged at the REAR edge of the base bin.
- `latch_dial`: the signature big round rotary dial latch centered on
  the front face (photo 4): a disc (~7 cm) that rotates ~60° to release
  two hook tabs engaging the lid rim; slight dome, molded arrow/lock
  glyphs.
- `handle`: top fold-flat carry handle on the lid (U-shaped bail that
  lies in the lid recess and swings up 90°).
- Compartment dividers molded into trays; a few simple prop contents
  are NOT required (keep the box empty and clean).

## Articulation (sim-ready, Level-3 USD export)

- `lid_hinge`: revolute at the rear edge, 0 (closed) to ~110° open.
- `latch_dial_rotate`: revolute on the front dial, 0 (locked) to ~60°
  (released).
- `tray_deploy`: THE signature DOF — a single state property (0–1)
  driving the cantilever mechanism: scissor links rotate (revolute
  joints at bin/tray attachment points) and each tray translates
  up-and-outward while remaining level. Model the link joints properly
  (each scissor link a separate link with revolute joints); couple all
  of them to the one `tray_deploy` slider via drivers. Trays at
  deploy=0 nest fully inside the bin under the lid; at deploy=1 they
  stagger open like the photo, upper level clearing the lower.
- `handle_fold`: revolute, 0 (flat in recess) to 90° (carry).
- Root: `medicine_box`, root link `base_bin`, root_role `dynamic`.

Conventions (mandatory): joint state sliders ON THE ROOT LINK OBJECT,
clamped; drivers forced through a depsgraph evaluation after rig build;
all drivers `is_valid`.

## Demo animation — `demo_open` (muted NLA, on the root link)

1. Latch dial rotates to release.
2. Lid opens to full range.
3. `tray_deploy` sweeps 0 → 1 (trays scissor up and out, staying
   level), holds, returns.
4. Handle folds up, small hold, folds flat; lid closes; dial locks.

The tray deployment path must be collision-free: encode a staged sweep
probe (lid-open × tray_deploy sampled ≥ 50 steps, BVH overlap of trays,
links, bin, lid) as a verify script run in the commit gate. Trays must
NOT clip the bin rim, each other, or the open lid.

Rest state: closed (lid down, latch locked, trays nested, handle flat).

## Deterministic constraints

- Outer dims within ±10% of documented/derived values; tray widths fit
  inside the bin with 2–5 mm clearance.
- Joint limits as specified; `tray_deploy` coupling: both tray levels
  move symmetrically (left/right link angle skew ≤ 0.5°); trays remain
  level within 2° through deployment.
- Collision-free tray sweep (staged probe in commit gate);
  non-intersection at rest and fully open.
- Serenity blue body color check: base_bin material base color within a
  tolerance band of sRGB (179, 199, 214).
- All drivers valid; sliders on root link object.

## Materials / look

Serenity-blue opaque PP for bin/trays (soft satin finish, subtle molded
texture), transparent smoke-gray polycarbonate lid (transmission with
light gray tint), lighter serenity-white dial/handle/links, molded-in
glyphs on the dial. Clean, modern, softly rounded — match the listing's
product-photography feel.

title: Workbench Power Scene
prompt:
Industrial workbench power setup — multi-object scene

Build a small industrial workbench scene with four objects. Model ONLY
these objects; no room, walls, floor slab, tools, or other props.

## Objects and layout

Scene datum: floor plane at Z=0, origin centered under the workbench,
+Y toward the rear of the bench, +X to the viewer's right when facing the
bench front.

1. **Metal workbench with a fixture plate on top.**
   - A plain industrial steel workbench (leg frame + solid work top,
     roughly 1500 x 750 mm footprint, work surface at ~850 mm), centered
     at the origin.
   - A separate, smaller perforated fixture/tooling plate (roughly
     900 x 600 x 22 mm) resting centered on the bench top, carrying a
     regular grid of positioning holes (~40 mm pitch, ~12 mm bores,
     generated procedurally — never hole-by-hole).
   - The bench and the plate are separate parts of one workbench object.

2. **Bench power supply**, sitting on the fixture plate at the LEFT end
   (viewer's left, -X). Critically: it is rotated so its BACK side faces
   toward the fixture plate center (+X direction) — its rear panel, with a
   detachable-cord inlet (IEC C14 style socket), is what you see when
   looking across the plate. Its front controls face away (-X, off the
   plate's left edge).

3. **Heavy-duty industrial power strip** on the OTHER side of the fixture
   plate (viewer's right, +X end of the plate): a rugged multi-outlet
   strip (metal or heavy ABS housing, several grounded outlets on its top
   face, a lit rocker switch), lying on the plate near its right edge,
   oriented with its long axis along Y.

4. **Detachable AC power cable** (connective object — build LAST):
   a heavy-gauge cable with a molded IEC C13 connector on one end and a
   grounded plug on the other. It plugs into the power supply's rear
   inlet, LAYS ACROSS the fixture plate surface (a natural relaxed path
   with gentle slack curves, resting on the plate, passing between the
   two devices), and reaches the power strip, with its plug inserted into
   (or resting at) one of the strip's outlets.

## Relations that must hold

- The power supply rests flat on the fixture plate (no floating, no
  sinking into the plate).
- The power strip rests flat on the fixture plate.
- The cable rests ON the plate surface along its span — not floating
  above it, not passing through the plate, the devices, or itself.
- Nothing intersects anything else.

## Articulation

- The workbench, power supply, and power strip are static/fixedbase
  objects (no joints required).
- The AC cable carries the scene's articulation: a prismatic insertion
  DOF at the power-supply end (`ac_cable_iec_insertion`, unplugged →
  seated in the IEC inlet) so plug/unplug can be simulated. Range ~0 to
  0.06 m along the inlet axis; seated at 0.

## Appearance

Industrial and clean: brushed/bead-blasted aluminium fixture plate with
darker bores, powder-coated dark gray bench frame, steel bench top,
typical bench-supply styling (dark metal chassis), black rubberized power
strip with colored rocker switch, black cable with molded connectors.
info: Built by calling the asset agent one asset at a time — workbench, power supply, power strip, AC cable.
The room background is a live-rendered Gaussian-Splat.
The scene exports to USD and runs in Isaac Sim for robot simulation (see video).
